title: An update to: Tracking TAU in neurons: How to transfect and track exogenous TAU in primary neurons
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"lbfec","text":"Primary murine neurons have proved to be an essential tool for the general investigation of neuronal polarity, polarized TAU distribution, and TAU-based neuronal dysfunction in disease paradigms. However, mature primary neurons are notoriously difficult to transfect with non-viral approaches and are very sensitive to cytoskeletal manipulation and imaging. Furthermore, standard non-viral transfection techniques require the use of a supportive glia monolayer or high-density cultures, both of which interfere with microscopy. Here we provide a simple non-viral liposome-based transfection method that enables transfection of TAU in low levels comparable to endogenous TAU. This allows the investigation of e.g., distribution and trafficking of TAU, without affecting other cytoskeleton-based parameters such as microtubule density or microtubule-based transport. Using this protocol, we achieve a profound transfection efficiency, but avoid high overexpression rates. Importantly, this transfection method can be applied to neurons at different ages and is also suitable for very old cultures (up to 18 days in vitro). In addition, the protocol can be used in cultures without glial support and at suitable cell densities for microscopy-based single-cell analysis. In sum, this protocol has proven a reliable tool suitable for most microscopy-based approaches in our laboratory.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":1109,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dydrt","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"bkqqk","text":"Primary murine and rat neuronal cultures from the forebrain, especially from the developing cortex and hippocampus, are widely used to investigate neuronal cell maturation, including synaptogenesis and cell polarity. Using low-density cultures, differential axodendritic and synaptic distribution of the protein in question can be examined with high magnification light and fluorescence microscopes. Unfortunately, primary neurons are challenging to mature in vitro and notoriously difficult to transfect with increasing age and maturation using non-viral transfection systems. However, generating viral vectors is time-consuming, and titer control is unprecise, making the comparison of different constructs and avoiding overexpression especially difficult.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":457,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7hngf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7atqy","text":"Here we describe a protocol based on liposomal transfection, compatible with a standardized, reproducible, and economic culturing procedure for primary neurons. Avoiding overexpression is particularly important for the TAU protein, as even wildtype-TAU can over-stabilize and bundle microtubules, impair axonal transport, resulting in loss of spines and other detrimental effects (see, e.g., Thies \u0026 Mandelkow, 2007; Zempel et al., 2013). As TAU can interact with microtubules as well as the actin cytoskeleton, overexpression can induce a wide range of cytoskeletal disturbances, possibly masking more subtle signaling or pathology-related effects (Zempel et al. 2013; Biernat et al. 2002; Sharma et al. 2007; Whiteman et al. 2009; Zempel and Mandelkow 2014). In conclusion, lower expression levels are crucial to investigate specific effects of mutated or (pseudo)phosphorylated TAU and its isoforms in neuronal cultures (Zempel et al. 2013; Frandemiche et al. 2014; Hoover et al. 2010; Bachmann et al. 2021; Zempel et al. 2017; Bell et al. 2021). Using this protocol, we achieve a transfection efficiency of up to 10 % in young cells (significantly lower in mature neurons) but avoid high overexpression rates. Importantly, this transfection method can be performed with cultures for several weeks of age, in cultures without glial support, at suitable cell densities for microscopy-based single-cell analysis, and favors expression in neurons compared to glial cells.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rg2he","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

Expression efficiency decreases with the growing age of the primary neurons; nevertheless, with our protocol, cultures can be transfected even at an age above DIV18 (see Note 1). Plasmids","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eyxbu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"prywa","text":"\u003c/strong\u003eWe usually use CMV-promotor-based plasmids. To monitor transfection efficiency, tdTomato is a suitable volume marker since it is very bright and (at the expression levels of this protocol) well tolerated by the cells in our experience for at least 1 week (Zempel et al. 2017; Bachmann et al. 2021; Bell et al. 2021).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lot3c","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jflhl","text":"3. Transfection reagents","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"q5btg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hhwpy","text":"\u003c/strong\u003eAll reagents should be handled under sterile conditions.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dgyuv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rvsjz","text":"\u003col\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vgakb","text":"  \u003cli\u003eLipofectamine2000 (Thermofisher Scientific, 11668019).\u003c/li\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0squv","text":"  \u003cli\u003eUnsupplemented Neurobasal (Neurobasal) medium (Thermofisher Scientific, 21103-049), pre-heated at 37 °C and pre-incubated with 5 % CO2 (see Note 2).\u003c/li\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":146,"length":6}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tak9c","text":"  \u003cli\u003eSufficient amounts of conditioned medium (CM). CM refers to the growth medium in which the neurons are grown. Optimally, the CM should come from the same neuronal culture that will be transfected to influence the culture conditions as little as possible. If CM from other cultures is used, we prefer CM of cultures of a similar age. For quantitative comparative experiments, the same CM should be used for one experiment or technical replicate. In our experience, different batches of CM change both expression level and duration.\u003c/li\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"42jod","text":"\u003c/ol\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Primary neurons should be kept in a humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. These cells do not tolerate full medium exchange without glial support; thus, all steps should be conducted with the conditioned medium if not otherwise indicated. Volumes and amounts are given for 24-well and 6-well plates, but volumes can be adjusted according to the surface area (cm2) of the multi-well plates used.
Reduce the amount of conditioned medium (CM) per well to 0.5 ml (for 6-well plate: 1 ml) before you start the preparation of the transfection mix (see 3.2 ). Store the aspirated CM.
Place neurons back into the incubator for the preparation of the transfection mix.
Keep conditioned medium at 37° C under 5 % CO2 for later use; leftover media can be stored at -20 °C (see Note 3 ).

Prepare the following mixes. The total DNA amount should not exceed the values given here. For example, if two plasmids are co-transfected, take only half of the total DNA amount for each plasmid. Plasmid ratios can be adjusted depending on the used constructs and the experiment (see Note 4 ). If several wells are going to be transfected, upscale the mixes accordingly:
DNA-mix ( A ):
For one 6-Well: Mix 1.0 µg of plasmid (see Note 4 ) and 60 µl fresh, unsupplemented Neurobasal medium.
Lipofectamine2000-mix ( B ):
For one 6-well: Mix 1.5 µl of Lipofectamine2000 with 60 µl fresh, unsupplemented Neurobasal medium.
Vortex and shortly spin down both mixtures (both <1 second).
Incubate mix B for 5 minutes at room temperature, mix B is stable for at least 1 hour, presumably longer (not tested).
Mix A and B in a 1:1 ratio. This mixture is referred to as the transfection mix.
Incubate the transfection mix for 30 minutes at room temperature (see Note 5 ). Avoid vortexing and centrifuging at steps 9-11, which will influence the liposome constitution.
Before transfection, take cells with the adjusted amount of conditioned medium (see 3.1.1 ) out of the incubator.
Add the whole transfection mix (30 µl for a 24-well) dropwise on top of the conditioned medium of the well to be transfected, from about 1.5- 2 cm distance.
After adding the transfection reagent to all desired wells, gently swirl the whole plate about 3-5 times (only to achieve homogenous distribution, do not stir or aspirate).
Place the cells immediately back into the incubator (see Note 6 ).
Incubate cells for 3 hours with the transfection mix in the incubator. The time can be adjusted to 1-6 hours depending on the desired expression level and the age of the neurons (see Note 7 ).
After the incubation time, wash cells gently two times with pre-warmed (37 °C), 5 % CO2 pH-equilibrated and unsupplemented Neurobasal medium (see Note 2 ).
Place pre-warmed (37 °C) conditioned medium (collected from the same culture before, see 3.1 ) back onto the cells (see Note 2 ).
Place cells immediately back into the incubator.
Allow expression for at least 2-3 days for young (<DIV14) and 5-6 days for old (>DIV18) cells for intermediate expressing proteins, such as wildtype TAU (see Note 8 ).

The expression efficiency of this protocol is sufficient for many approaches but does not compare to other expression systems (especially viral-based strategies). Thus, if you want to test the transfection efficiency, investigate it using a bright fluorescent protein without or with little physiological or pathological activity in neurons. For example, expression of an untagged fluorescent protein such as tdTomato can be observed as soon as 1 day after transfection in young (up to DIV14) neurons, while this may take up to 2-3 days (peak expression usually around 5-6 days) in older neurons. If an investigation of mature neurons (DIV14-18) is desired, we recommend transfection at the age of DIV10-14. Transfection of cells DIV18 and older is possible and feasible, but in this case, cells should be allowed to express the protein for 5-6 days, particularly for functional proteins. Note that independent of the age of the neurons, cells that highly overexpress any transfected protein may die after 2-5 days simply due to overexpression. Thus, analyzing only brightly fluorescent cells 1-2 days after transfection will yield biased results because, at that time, only soon-to-die cells will express the transfected proteins in high amounts. All media that come in direct undiluted contact with the cells should always be maintained at 37°C and equilibrated with 5% CO2. The Neurobasal medium contains the pH-sensitive dye phenol red, which should be bright red. If the medium is purple, do not use it. Neurobasal and conditioned medium will change their pH within minutes under normal atmospheric conditions (i.e., without 5% CO2) and become purple. In that case, place them in an incubator diffused with 5% CO2 until the medium is bright red. Sufficient experience with these media should be acquired before using media without phenol red. We have not observed any differences in viability or transfection efficiency depending on the presence of phenol red. However, some live-imaging procedures require the use of phenol red-free medium because of significant autofluorescence in basically all wave-lengths of commonly used microscopes. When conditioning, consider using a phenol red containing medium as control. The conditioned medium can be stored at -20°C for future use. This is particularly useful when this transfection protocol is used for multi-well plates with small volumes; since then aspirated amounts may be insufficient for further processing and growing of cells. Before use, pre-warm conditioned medium to 37°C and keep it under 5% CO2 until the medium color becomes bright red. Several plasmids (we use up to three with excellent results) can be mixed at different ratios, depending on the desired expression efficacy, for simultaneous expression of several proteins. If such a co-transfection is desired, plasmids must be mixed before mixing the DNA-mix with the Lipofectamine-mix. Expression efficacy can easily be adjusted by changing the amount of plasmid DNA. As fluorescent proteins not tagged to functional proteins usually have very high expression efficiencies, we often reduce the DNA amount to half the suggested amount. For TAU protein, 0.25 µg per well of a 24-well plate seems to be a good compromise between visibility with decent (eGFP, YFP, Citrine) fluorescent protein tags and not too much expression. The incubation time of the transfection mix can be varied from 20-45 minutes, but the best results are obtained after 30 minutes. Adding the transfection mix to the multi-well plate should be kept below 3 minutes, as the reduced amount of conditioned medium quickly alkalizes under a common laboratory air-flow clean bench. It is essential to add the transfection mix dropwise Transfection time can be varied to adjust the expression level. 3 hours of transfection time works for all ages of neurons and is particularly suited for the expression of TAU protein to near-physiological levels in already polarized and synaptically active primary neurons aged DIV10-14. In the case of very young neurons (DIV3-7), which are efficiently transfected and express high amounts of the transfected protein, we decrease transfection time to 1 hour, in the case of old neurons (>DIV15), which are more difficult to transfect and express only low levels of the transfected protein, we increase the transfection time up to 6 hours. We do not observe increased cell death up to 6 hours of transfection time. In the case of wildtype TAU protein, depending on the type of analysis, the expression should be at least 2-3 days in mature primary neurons, due to two reasons: i) TAU protein propagates very slowly into the axon, e.g., after 24 hours of expression it has not even reached the axonal growth cone (in DIV10 cortical neurons), which means that at least for some TAU proteins a certain amount of time (more than 1 day) is required to obtain homogenous or physiological-like distribution throughout the whole cell (Li et al., 2011, Zempel 2017, Bachmann 2021). ii) It is certainly possible to attain sufficient expression even after 1 day of transfection. However, cells expressing such high amounts of protein will soon succumb due to unspecific high expression-related toxicity. Therefore, well-designed controls are necessary in this case, e.g., if specific TAU protein-related toxicity will be investigated. The ratio of fresh Neurobasal versus conditioned medium can be increased up to 1:1 for cells younger than DIV10 but will negatively affect cell viability, especially for older cells.
For one 24-Well: Mix 0.25 µg of plasmid and 15 µl fresh, unsupplemented Neurobasal medium.
For one 24-well: Mix 0.375 µl of Lipofectamine2000 with 15 µl fresh, unsupplemented Neurobasal medium.
